claim: Obsidian Properties are typed YAML metadata stored at the top of notes; supported types are text, list, number, checkbox, date, and date & time, with tags as a special list-typed property.
quote: Properties have types (e.g. text, list, date, number, checkbox) making data entry faster and more accurate. Tags is a special property type exclusively assigned to the tags property.

claim: A given property name can only carry one type across the whole vault, which is what makes consistent filtering and querying possible.
quote: A property name can only have one type across the entire vault. Once a property is assigned a type (e.g., status as Text), all notes using status must use the Text type.

claim: Obsidian 1.9.0 (May 2025) shipped Bases as a core plugin that turns notes into database-like table views driven by YAML properties, introducing a new .base file format.
quote: Bases lets you create custom table views to visualize and interact with data in your vault. This is an early beta. We expect many changes and improvements to Bases over the coming months.

claim: Bases queries the same seven Properties types plus implicit fields like file path/folder, supports three-component filters (property/operator/value) with and/or/not, and currently only renders table views; inline Dataview-style properties are not supported.
quote: The plugin works with seven property types: Text, List, Number, Checkbox, Date, and Date & Time. It also supports implicit properties, like file path or folder. Inline properties aren't supported.

claim: Dataview reads YAML frontmatter and inline `key:: value` fields equivalently, so authors can mix both; inline fields use double colons and can sit in lists, tasks, or sentences via [bracket:: value] syntax.
quote: Inline fields use a double colon :: between key and value, while YAML frontmatter fields use a single colon. You can embed metadata inside sentences or multiple fields on the same line using bracket syntax like [rating:: 9].

claim: Practitioners are migrating off Dataview inline fields onto YAML frontmatter because Dataview is largely unmaintained and heavy queries cause keystroke lag, with Bases and Datacore positioned as the longer-term query layer; Meta Bind is used to keep YAML values editable inline.
quote: lag in keystrokes when typing into pages with heavy Dataview queries... Dataview treats inline fields and YAML frontmatter the same. The Meta Bind plugin became essential, allowing fields stored in YAML to display inline.

claim: A maintainable Bases-era vault depends on a central master property schema and a mandatory `type` property on every queryable note, which acts as the primary discriminator each Base filters on.
quote: Establishing a consistent, vault-wide Universal Data Language through a master property schema is therefore not optional... The cornerstone of a schema is a mandatory type property for every note intended for use with Bases.

claim: As of Obsidian 1.9 the singular keys `tag`, `alias`, and `cssclass` are deprecated in favour of plural list forms `tags`, `aliases`, `cssclasses`, and ISO date/datetime formats are recommended for reliable sorting.
quote: As of Obsidian 1.9, the old singular properties tag, alias, and cssclass are deprecated. Use the plural forms and make them lists. Prefer ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD) and ISO datetime (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM) for reliable sorting.

claim: Obsidian autocompletes property names from existing vault keys when you type, which is the cheapest mechanism for keeping a schema consistent and avoiding duplicate keys (status vs progress, due_date vs deadline).
quote: Obsidian automatically keeps track of every property you use across your vault. When you go to add a property to a new note, it will suggest existing property keys, helping you avoid typos and duplicates.

claim: In 1.9.1 Obsidian renamed Bases built-in functions from snake_case to camelCase to align with the Obsidian API surface, an early sign that Bases formulas are stabilising as a public schema.
quote: renamed the built-in functions for Bases from snake_case to camelCase, a naming schema considered more consistent with the Obsidian API.
